data.title: Bitcoin Tops $78,000 As Strait Reopens And Gains 3.8%
data.description:
Bitcoin's (BTC-USD) latest move is beginning to test investor conviction as prices push toward the upper end of the recent range. The cryptocurrency climbed above $78,000 for the first time since Feb. 3, rising as much as 3.8% to $78,155 following a series of remarks from the US and Iran that suggested tensions in the Middle East could be easing. Iran's statement that the Strait of Hormuz is now completely open for commercial traffic appeared to act as a key trigger, coinciding with a broader shift into risk assets, as equities advanced while oil and the dollar moved lower.

The strength extended across digital assets, with Ether gaining 3.3% and XRP rising 2.4%, pointing to a broader improvement in sentiment rather than an isolated move in Bitcoin. Market participants have framed the reopening of the Strait as a meaningful development for global liquidity and confidence, though positioning data suggests investors remain cautious. Funding rates for perpetual futures contracts stayed negative, while traders continued to pay notable premiums for downside protection at $60,000 and $50,000, indicating that hedging demand remains elevated despite the recent price strength.

At the same time, several institutional developments could be providing incremental support. Strategy Inc. acquired $2.6 billion in Bitcoin over the past two weeks, which has been cited as a factor underpinning the market. Charles Schwab announced plans to launch spot crypto trading this year and indicated that clients could allocate as much as 8.8% of portfolios to Bitcoin. In parallel, Goldman Sachs filed for a Bitcoin ETF, marking its first direct move into the space, while Morgan Stanley recently launched its own Bitcoin-tracking ETF. Even with these catalysts, traders suggest that sustained institutional flows and continued geopolitical clarity may be required before Bitcoin can break out of its range with stronger conviction.

data.title: US Weighs Boosting Namibia Uranium Imports As AI Power Demand Rises
data.description:
US officials are increasingly positioning uranium as a strategic lever in the evolving energy landscape, with Namibia emerging as a potential supply partner as demand accelerates. Ambassador John Giordano indicated that the US is evaluating the prospect of increasing uranium imports from the southern African nation, framing the resource as a key area of alignment between the two countries. He pointed to the role nuclear energy could play in powering hyperscale data centers tied to artificial intelligence, suggesting that uranium demand may rise alongside that buildout.

This interest is developing within a broader shift in energy policy, where rising electricity consumption from data centers is reshaping how governments think about power generation. Under US President Donald Trump, the policy mix has emphasized coal, natural gas, and nuclear as potential solutions to meet growing demand. Namibia, currently the world's third-largest uranium producer behind Kazakhstan and Canada, exported 633 metric tons of uranium to the US in 2024, while China imported significantly higher volumes, which could indicate a gap Washington may look to narrow over time.

At the same time, US engagement in Namibia appears to extend beyond imports toward building a more integrated energy and minerals corridor across southern Africa. Discussions involving financing from US institutions, including the Development Finance Corp., Export-Import Bank, and Trade and Development Agency, are ongoing, alongside interest from private investors. US companies such as Chevron Corp. , Exxon Mobil Corp. , Baker Hughes Co. , and Halliburton Co. have expanded in Namibia following offshore oil discoveries in 2022, while future access to uranium and other critical minerals could depend on developing new mining projects, particularly given the current dominance of Chinese operators in the sector.

data.title: Strategy (MSTR) stock jumps 15% as Bitcoin rally pushes holdings to profit
data.description:
Shares of Strategy (previously known as Microstrategy) surged sharply on Friday, tracking a strong rally in Bitcoin that pushed the company’s massive cryptocurrency holdings back into profitable territory.

The stock climbed 16.25% to its intraday high, although it is trading 10% higher at the time of writing, extending a five-day rally that has put it on track for a weekly gain of more than 30%.

The move came as Bitcoin rose nearly 4% in the past 24 hours, briefly approaching $78,000 and trading above $77,400.

Bitcoin rally lifts Strategy’s holdings

The surge in Bitcoin prices has had a direct impact on Strategy’s balance sheet, given its large exposure to the digital asset. The company has accumulated more than 780,000 Bitcoin, with an average purchase price estimated at $75,580 according to data from BitcoinTreasuries.

Earlier this year, Bitcoin had fallen below Strategy’s average acquisition cost, placing its holdings under pressure. The latest rally has reversed that trend, pushing the company’s Bitcoin portfolio back into the green by about 3.14%, or roughly $1.8 billion in gains.

With Bitcoin now above its average cost basis, Strategy’s treasury strategy is once again delivering positive returns, reinforcing investor enthusiasm for the stock.

The company’s holdings are currently valued at approximately $56 billion, underscoring the scale of its bet on the cryptocurrency market.

Leverage to Bitcoin drives stock gains

Strategy’s stock performance remains closely tied to movements in Bitcoin prices, amplifying gains during rallies and losses during downturns. The company’s sensitivity to Bitcoin is evident in its balance sheet dynamics.

For every $1,000 increase in Bitcoin’s price, Strategy adds about $780 million to the value of its holdings. This leverage has become a key driver of investor interest, particularly during periods of strong upward momentum in the crypto market.

If Bitcoin were to return to its previous all-time high of $126,000, the value of Strategy’s Bitcoin holdings would rise to approximately $98.4 billion, without the company needing to acquire additional coins.

The latest price surge has also fueled strong retail interest in the stock.

Positioning among major Bitcoin holders

Strategy remains one of the largest corporate holders of Bitcoin globally and is approaching the scale of major institutional products such as BlackRock’s iShares Bitcoin ETF in total holdings.

Its aggressive accumulation strategy, backed by co-founder Michael Saylor, has made the company a proxy for Bitcoin exposure in equity markets.

The recent rally highlights both the potential upside and inherent volatility of this approach. While rising Bitcoin prices can significantly boost Strategy’s valuation, the company’s fortunes remain heavily dependent on the cryptocurrency’s performance.

As Bitcoin continues to trade near recent highs, investors are likely to keep a close watch on further price movements and their implications for Strategy’s balance sheet and stock trajectory.

data.title: Canadian Securities Exchange Reports March 2026 Performance Figures
data.description:
Toronto, Ontario--(Newsfile Corp. - April 17, 2026) - The Canadian Securities Exchange ("CSE" or "the Exchange") today announced market statistics for March 2026.

March 2026 Operating Statistics

  • Trading volume of CSE listed securities totaled 1.6 billion shares;
  • Trading value of CSE listed securities was $952 million;
  • CSE issuers completed 112 financings that raised an aggregate $270 million; and
  • The CSE welcomed listings from six new companies, including two fundamental changes of existing issuers, bringing total listed securities to 735 as at March 31, 2026.

"The Canadian Securities Exchange continues to attract new listings from incredibly bright and innovative entrepreneurs around the world," said Richard Carleton, CEO of the CSE. "During the month of March, we added new listings from an Israeli company harnessing nanotechnology to reduce diabetic eye disease (Rise Nano Optics), and an American company utilizing artificial intelligence to try to rapidly connect patients with doctors (UBERDOC Health Technologies). We are delighted that the leaders of these companies chose to list on the CSE, recognizing that it provides the best option for them to pursue their capital market goals."

What's On at the CSE

The CSE is participating in the latest FISD forum in Toronto on April 29. FISD events focus on the evolution of financial information and key trends shaping the finance industry. This edition will feature a panel on artificial intelligence titled AI in the Age of Compliance: Controlling Data as Models Grow Smarter. Registration is free for members and $500 for non-members.

More than 20,000 people will be gathering for the Web Summit Vancouver on May 11-14, and the CSE is pleased to be attending. The conference connects investors and media with leading technology start-up companies from around the world and features an extensive line-up of expert speakers and panel discussions focusing on key trends in the sector. This is the second consecutive year in which Vancouver has hosted this event, following a successful debut in 2025.

In conjunction with the Web Summit Vancouver, the CSE is excited to host its second annual Web Summit Tech Connect. The special event will feature presentations from senior executives of a select group of CSE-listed technology companies, and other industry experts. There will also be valuable networking opportunities between members of the capital market and tech communities.

The Critical Minerals Institute (CMI) is hosting its latest summit on May 13-14 in Toronto. The CSE is sponsoring and attending the timely conference, which will explore how defense budgets, national security mandates, and industrial-policy incentives are impacting the critical minerals sector. The summit will include keynote presentations, interactive panels, Q&A sessions and a networking reception. Several CSE issuers are participating in the conference, including Allied Critical Metals Inc. (ACM), American Tungsten Corp. (TUNG), Antimony Resources Corp. (ATMY), Appia Rare Earths & Uranium Corp. (API), and Renforth Resources Inc. (RFR).

The CSE is pleased to be returning to the Current Trends in Mining Finance Conference in New York on May 19-20. The 11th annual edition of the conference will connect mining, finance and engineering executives and will explore many distinct aspects of mining finance through panels, keynote speeches, fireside chats and receptions. Topics of discussion will include types of financing, commodity-specific financing outlooks, and the impact on financing from changes in government policy, technology and legal frameworks.

New Listings in March 2026

Gold Orogen Resources Corp. (OROG) - Fundamental Change

Rise Nano Optics Ltd. (EYE)

UBERDOC Health Technologies Corp. (APPT) - Fundamental Change

Rockbridge Resources Inc. (ROCB)

Allied Strategic Resource Corp. (ASR)

ICG Silver & Gold Ltd. (ICG)

About the Canadian Securities Exchange:

The Canadian Securities Exchange is a rapidly growing exchange invested in working with entrepreneurs, innovators and disruptors to access public capital markets in Canada. The Exchange's efficient operating model, advanced technology and competitive fee structure help its listed issuers of all sectors and sizes minimize their cost of capital and enhance global liquidity.

Our client-centric approach and corresponding products and services ensure businesses have the support they need to confidently realize their vision.

The CSE offers global investors access to an innovative collection of growing and mature companies.

STAY CONNECTED WITH THE CSE

data.title: Tax Refunds Are Higher. That's Only a Fraction of the Story. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Daniel Bunn

About the author: Daniel Bunn is president and CEO of the Tax Foundation.

It's not every year that politicians enthusiastically promote Tax Day. But it's also not every tax year that Congress and the president are able to get sweeping tax reforms like the One Big Beautiful Bill Act across the finish line.

President Donald Trump and GOP leaders in Congress used the occasion of filing day to talk up their "working families tax cuts" in press appearances in Washington and around the country this week. Trump made a stop in Las Vegas on Thursday and has a planned visit to Phoenix Friday to tout the larger refunds that Americans received.

The president is right that average refunds this filing season were larger for many. The average refund this year is $3,571, up roughly 11%, or $350 , from the year prior. But higher prices caused by his administration's other policies and actions will eat away at those potential savings before the refund check even hits Americans' bank accounts. And the tax code is still far too complex.

Trump has celebrated that his party delivered the "biggest tax cuts ever, " and that new deductions and carve-outs, such as auto-loan interest payment deductions and no tax on overtime or tips, are benefiting the American working class. Only those in select industries are eligible (The Internal Revenue Service didn't release its guidance for who qualifies — hospitality workers, tour guides, and even wedding photographers — until just days before the filing deadline.) The White House says more than six million filers have claimed the deduction.

It is still too early to tell how implementing many small tax breaks, as opposed to a few big ones, will play out across the economy. But they certainly add to the tax code's complexity and the administrative burden on tax filers.

Americans spent almost 7.1 billion hours complying with IRS tax filing and reporting requirements in 2025. That is equal to 3.4 million full-time workers doing nothing but filing out IRS forms for a year. The tax act's complex rules surely added to those costs this year.

It is fair and appropriate to recognize the growth-oriented changes that Republicans made to the tax code last year. The tax act staved off a tax increase that was set to hit nearly 62% of filers. And with a larger standard deduction and permanently lower rates for individuals and joint filers, the total amount refunded ran ahead of the past filing season by tens of billions of dollars.

But in the same way that tax refunds are only one part of the tax ecosystem, tax policy is only one part of the economy. Other parts of Trump's economic agenda have, without question, chipped away at the larger refunds Americans are seeing. The reality is that on Tax Day 2026, the cost of day-to-day life was higher and more financially tenuous than it was a year prior.

Trump's tariff regime is directly increasing the price of essentials. Tariffs have increased retail prices by seven percentage points over the past year. Clothes prices have jumped by 17.5%. Gas prices are edging toward $4 a gallon on average, putting upward pressure on inflation, which is now at its highest rate in three years. Consumer sentiment dropped nearly 11% in the first month of the war in Iran.

An extra few hundred dollars landing in Americans' wallets from once-a-year tax refunds won't ease that economic strain or meaningfully improve the consumer outlook.

Smarter tax policy, aimed at further simplifying the tax code while boosting economic growth, can. The appetite for this on Capitol Hill seems limited, if it exists at all.

Leading Democrats in Congress have recently introduced bills that would create further carve-outs and more headaches. Sen. Chris Van Hollen (D.,-M. D.), for instance, wants to create a new "minimum tax" calculation for low-earners and a new "alternative maximum tax" on high-earners. Rep. Nancy Mace (R.,-S. C.) proposes creating a new savings account for first-time home buyers that would further complicate the web of savings vehicles Americans already have. These types of bills, even if well intentioned, are a sign many are taking the wrong lessons from tax reform.

Bigger refunds are an easy political sell. But Congress should stay focused on streamlining the tax code and enhancing pieces of the tax code that will stimulate real growth.

They should improve upon the investment provisions in the tax act by making all the expensing provisions permanent, for instance. Under OBBBA, businesses can fully and permanently write off the cost of investment in research and development and equipment and machinery. But that isn't true for new construction. Congress should make write-offs for investments in structures permanent, too.

And the president should end the trade war that he escalated in 2025. Getting rid of tariffs would save the average American $400 a year — more than the average American received in extra tax refunds this year. A swift end to the war would help, too.

Washington knows what is driving up prices and which policies are making filing more burdensome, costly, and time-consuming. Tax season will come again next year. The question is, will lawmakers make it one actually worth celebrating?

Guest commentaries like this one are written by authors outside the Barron's newsroom. They reflect the perspective and opinions of the authors. Submit feedback and commentary pitches to ideas@barrons.com .

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Tesla Seeks Engineers For Taiwan Terafab Targeting 2nm AI Chip Production
data.description:
Tesla appears to be moving deeper into semiconductor manufacturing, with recent job postings pointing to early staffing for its Terafab AI chip complex in Taiwan. The company is hiring multiple senior process engineers, along with roles in process integration and yield and metrology, which could suggest foundational work is underway for a facility that may play a larger role in Tesla's long-term compute strategy. This hiring activity comes as demand for advanced chips continues to expand, and Tesla's approach could allow it to build more control over a critical layer of its technology stack.

In its job descriptions, Tesla outlines Terafab as a vertically integrated semiconductor factory operating at an unprecedented scale, bringing logic, memory, packaging, testing, and lithography mask production together under one roof. The facility is described as being optimized for rapid iteration and maximum compute density per square foot, which could indicate a focus on improving efficiency and accelerating development cycles. If executed as described, this level of integration may help Tesla reduce dependence on external suppliers, although semiconductor manufacturing remains highly complex and execution timelines are not yet defined.

The roles also reference work across three chip families, including edge-inference processors, space-hardened chips for orbital satellites, and high-bandwidth memory. Mentions of 2nm-class chip dimensions and fabrication steps such as lithography, etching, thin films, and chemical mechanical planarization suggest Tesla is targeting advanced process technologies. This initiative could potentially extend Tesla's capabilities beyond automotive applications into broader AI and aerospace compute markets, though the scale and timing of such expansion remain uncertain.

data.title: Farmhouse 2025 10-K: Revenue $0.000623M, EPS $(0.02)
data.description:
Farmhouse reported fiscal 2025 revenue of $0.000623 million and a basic and diluted net loss per share of $(0.02), reflecting a steep decline in legacy licensing revenue and a smaller net loss year-over-year driven by a gain on extinguishment of debt offset by certain write-offs. The company recorded a net loss of $(0.393266) million while shifting strategic focus toward a digital asset treasury initiative. Reduced operating activity from its former major customer contributed to the revenue decline.

Financial Highlights

  • Revenue: $0.000623 million (decline from $4,154 in 2024)
  • Gross margin: $0.000498 million
  • Loss from operations: $(0.410442) million
  • Net loss: $(0.393266) million
  • Basic and diluted net loss per share: $(0.02)

Business Highlights

  • Revenue trend: Legacy licensing customer activity wound down, reducing revenue to $623 in 2025 from $4,154 in 2024.
  • Strategic shift: Pivot to a digital asset treasury (DAT) initiative and formation of Farmhouse Treasury to evaluate crypto and gold strategies.
  • Operational activity: Maintained public company infrastructure and professional services while reducing personnel and consulting costs.
  • Market engagement: Established institutional custody relationship with BitGo and pursued exploratory counterparty discussions for digital-asset structures.
  • Capital strategy: Actively pursuing financings and strategic transactions to fund operations and support the DAT initiative.

Original SEC Filing: FARMHOUSE, INC. /NV [ FMHS ] - 10-K - Apr. 17, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: AI Startups Capture $242B As Global Funding Hits $300B In Q1 2026
data.description:
The artificial intelligence investment cycle is not just holding upit is accelerating in a way that could reshape capital allocation globally. Crunchbase data shows investors deployed $300B across 6,000 startups in the first quarter of 2026, representing a surge of more than 150% both quarter-over-quarter and year-over-year. What stands out is the concentration: roughly $242B, or about 80% of that total, was directed into AI companies, a sharp increase from the prior peak of 55% seen in Q1 2025. The scale of this shift suggests investors are increasingly willing to concentrate capital into AI as a core productivity driver, rather than treating it as just another emerging theme.

That conviction is translating into record-setting funding rounds and valuations. Microsoft -backed OpenAI raised $122B at an $852B valuation, marking the largest funding round on record. Anthropic, backed by Amazon and Google , secured $30B, while xAI, now part of SpaceX, raised $20B with strategic backing from Nvidia and Cisco . These companies are scaling aggressively while remaining unprofitable, yet continue to attract significant capital and are targeting potential IPOs, possibly as soon as this year.

At the same time, the macro backdrop could introduce some friction. Rising geopolitical tensions, higher oil prices, and renewed inflation concerns may lead to higher interest rates, which could weigh on broader venture activity and keep IPO markets somewhat subdued in the near term. Even so, AI appears positioned to remain a standout area for global investors, alongside defense tech, space tech, and cybersecurity. The key question for investors is not whether capital continues to flow into AI, but whether the pace and scale of current funding can ultimately translate into sustainable returns as these companies move closer to public markets.

data.title: Ecopetrol expects test results on Copoazu-1 deepwater well in late June
data.description:
Colombia's state oil producer Ecopetrol expects to have final results for tests on the Copoazu-1 exploratory natural gas well, run jointly with Brazil's Petrobras, at the end of June, Ecopetrol's interim CEO Juan Carlos Hurtado said on Friday.

  • Ecopetrol and Petrobras confirmed the discovery of the well, in Colombia's Caribbean, last month.

  • The well is located in Block GUA-OFF-0 in deepwater Colombia, about 36 kilometers (22.37 miles) from shore.

  • "We are finalizing preparations to conduct tests at the end of this month, and we expect to have results from these tests by late June, enabling us to deliver positive news to the country and to our sector," Hurtado said at a conference held by industry group Naturgas in Cartagena.

  • The well's discovery confirms the existence of a gas basin in the northern part of the country—spanning the entire Caribbean coast, Hurtado added.

  • Ecopetrol holds a 55.6% stake in the project, while Petrobras owns the remaining 44.4% and acts as its operator.
